narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid LSAT score of 38—above the typical 36.7 average—and an undergraduate GPA of 3.0, which is slightly below the average of 3.21. However, the university's admission model, trained on data from thousands of past students' bar exam outcomes, predicted a 76% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on how your academic history stacks up against patterns seen in students who succeeded or struggled with the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who typically pass the bar. Nearly as important was your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the average of roughly 5.7, reinforcing a pattern of early and sustained below-average class standing that the model associates strongly with bar exam risks. On a more positive note, your stronger-than-average LSAT score worked against this trend, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success and showing your potential in standardized testing. Overall, these early and later grade deciles outweighed your LSAT strength, as they mirror the profiles of many students who faced bar challenges, unlike the higher-decile performers who dominate the passer group. While factors like your full-time status—common among 93% of students—and family income didn't sway things much here, the model saw your academic deciles as the biggest red flags. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential as a Black man pursuing law, but a data-driven flag based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or targeted GPA improvement, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas—many students in similar situations have turned predictions around with focused effort. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews that weigh your LSAT edge more heavily or seek bar prep resources early. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
